senses.definition: to try to do something again, especially after it has failed
examples: If the upload fails, just retry after a few seconds.
The player can retry the level as many times as needed.

senses.definition: to examine a legal case or a person in court again
examples: The court decided to retry the case after new evidence appeared.
He was retried in a higher court the following year.

senses.definition: present participle of retry: trying to do something again after it has failed
examples: The system is retrying the connection automatically.
She kept retrying until the program finally installed.

senses.definition: third person singular of ret: to soak plant stems such as flax to separate useful fibers from the rest of the plant
examples: The farmer rets the flax in large tanks before it is processed.
She rets the hemp in the river for several days to loosen the fibers.

senses.definition: a Greek white or rosé wine flavored with pine resin
examples: We ordered a carafe of retsina with our seafood dinner.
Some visitors find the strong taste of retsina unusual at first.

senses.definition: plural of retsina: glasses or bottles of Greek wine flavored with pine resin
examples: They sampled several different retsinas at the wine festival.
The shop sells both local whites and imported retsinas.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of ret: soaked to separate plant fibers from the rest of the stem
examples: The flax was retted in ponds before being processed into linen.
Properly retted hemp produces long, strong fibers.

senses.definition: a person or machine that rets plant stems to obtain fibers
examples: The retter monitors the flax tanks during the retting process.
A modern retter can handle several tons of straw each day.

senses.definition: plural of rettery: places where flax or other plants are retted
examples: In the past, many villages had small retteries near the river.
Industrial retteries now process large amounts of fiber crops.

senses.definition: a place where plant stems such as flax are retted to produce fibers
examples: The old rettery by the canal has been turned into a museum.
Odors from the rettery could be smelled across the valley.

senses.definition: the process of soaking plant stems so that useful fibers can be separated from the rest of the plant
examples: Retting is an important step in producing linen from flax.
Water retting usually gives finer fibers than field retting.

senses.definition: present participle of ret: soaking plant stems to free the fibers
examples: Farmers are retting the flax in shallow ponds this week.
They are retting the hemp in the field to reduce costs.

senses.definition: an Italian title for the head of a university or other institution, similar to rector
examples: The rettore welcomed the new students at the opening ceremony.
She was appointed rettore of the university after many years as a professor.

senses.definition: archaic past participle of return
examples: The goods, once rettorn, could not be sold as new.
All monies rettorn were recorded in the ledger.

senses.definition: an archaic form of rectory, meaning the house of a rector
examples: The old rettory stood at the edge of the village green.
Generations of clergy had lived in the stone rettory.

senses.definition: to fit again with a tube or tubes, or to replace used tubes with new ones
examples: The engineers had to retube the boiler after the inspection.
He plans to retube the old guitar amplifier to improve the sound.

senses.definition: to tuck something in again, especially fabric or bedding
examples: She had to retuck the sheets after the child woke up.
He stood to retuck his shirt into his trousers.

senses.definition: to tumble or fall again
examples: The blocks retumbled across the floor when the table was bumped.
He slipped on the ice and retumbled down the steps.

senses.definition: a process or state of swelling again or becoming larger after shrinking
examples: Doctors noted a retumescence of the tissues after the treatment stopped.
The slight retumescence of the river level worried local officials.

senses.definition: to blunt or make something less sharp
examples: Centuries of use had retunded the edge of the stone step.
The constant friction slowly retunds the metal point.

senses.definition: made blunt or less sharp
examples: A retunded chisel can slip and cause an accident.
The blade was so retunded that it barely cut the wood.

senses.definition: present participle of retund: making something less sharp
examples: Sand is slowly retunding the rocks on the shore.
Continuous use is retunding the knife's cutting edge.

senses.definition: third person singular of retund: makes something blunt or less sharp
examples: Friction quickly retunds the end of the pencil.
Constant impact retunds the hammer's striking face.

senses.definition: to change the tuning of an instrument, device, or system so that it works at a new setting or note
examples: You need to retune the guitar before the concert.
Viewers had to retune their televisions after the channel changes.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of retune: changed to a new tuning or setting
examples: The piano was retuned just before the performance.
We retuned the radio to a different station.

senses.definition: third person singular of retune: changes the tuning or setting of something
examples: He retunes his violin before every rehearsal.
The device automatically retunes when you move to a new area.

senses.definition: the act of changing the tuning or setting of something
examples: A quick retuning of the piano was needed during the break.
Digital retuning of TV sets caused confusion for some viewers.

senses.definition: present participle of retune: changing the tuning or setting of something
examples: She is retuning the violin between pieces.
They are retuning the system to improve the signal.

senses.definition: to put a turban back on someone or on yourself
examples: He stopped to returban himself before entering the temple.
The actor had to returban quickly between scenes.

senses.definition: to lay new grass on an area of ground
examples: The club decided to returf the football pitch over the summer.
We will need to returf the lawn after the construction work.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of returf: covered again with new grass
examples: The stadium pitch was completely returfed last year.
We returfed the garden after installing the new patio.

senses.definition: a person or company that lays new turf on the ground
examples: We hired a professional returfer to fix the damaged lawn.
The stadium uses specialist returfers for the pitch.

senses.definition: the activity of putting new grass on an area of ground
examples: Returfing of the main pitch will take three weeks.
The park will be closed during returfing.

senses.definition: present participle of returf: putting new grass on an area of ground
examples: They are returfing the training ground this month.
We are returfing the garden after the building work.

senses.definition: third person singular of returf: puts new grass on an area of ground
examples: The club returbs the pitch every few years.
The company returbs damaged lawns after events.

senses.definition: to go or come back to a place or person
examples: She will return home next week.
We returned to the hotel after the concert.

senses.definition: to give, send, or take something back to the person or place it came from
examples: I must return these books to the library.
Please return the form by Friday.

senses.definition: the act of going or coming back to a place or person
examples: We are looking forward to your return.
There is no chance of a return to the old system.

senses.definition: the amount of profit or benefit you get from something, such as an investment or effort
examples: This fund offers a good return on your money.
We need to see results and a return on our investment.

senses.definition: plural of returnability: the degrees to which things such as containers or products can be returned for reuse or recycling
examples: The report compares the returnabilities of different types of packaging.
Policies aim to increase the returnabilities of beverage containers.

senses.definition: the quality of being able to be returned, especially for reuse, recycling, or refund
examples: Designing for returnability can reduce waste and costs.
Bottles with higher returnability are better for the environment.

senses.definition: that can or must be given or sent back
examples: The bottles are returnable and you get a small refund.
All goods are returnable within 30 days with a receipt.

senses.definition: items, especially bottles or containers, that can be taken back for reuse or refund
examples: He took a bag of returnables to the recycling center.
Some stores pay cash for returnables.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of return: went or came back
examples: She returned from London yesterday.
We returned to our seats after the break.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of return: gave or sent something back
examples: I returned the package because it was damaged.
She returned the call as soon as she could.

senses.definition: given, sent, or taken back
examples: The store keeps a list of returned items.
Returned mail was piling up on the desk.

senses.definition: a person who returns to a place, especially to their own country, after living or staying somewhere else
examples: The government offers support to returnees who start new businesses.
Many returnees find it hard to adjust after years abroad.

senses.definition: plural of returnee: people who return to a place, especially to their own country, after living or staying somewhere else
examples: Many returnees bring new skills and ideas back home.
The program helps returnees find jobs and housing.

senses.definition: a person who goes back to a place, job, or situation after being away
examples: The company offers training for career returners.
As a recent returner to the city, he was still finding his way around.

senses.definition: plural of returner: people who go back to a place, job, or situation after being away
examples: The course is designed for returners to the workplace.
Schools are seeing more adult returners in evening classes.

senses.definition: a person, especially a Jew from the former Soviet Union, who returns to their country of origin or to Israel
examples: The novel tells the story of a young returnik adjusting to life in Israel.
As a returnik, she felt caught between two cultures.

senses.definition: plural of returnik: people who return to their country of origin, especially Jews leaving the former Soviet Union
examples: The government created special programs to help returniks settle.
Many returniks struggled with language and cultural changes.

senses.definition: present participle of return: going or coming back to a place or person
examples: She is returning home after many years abroad.
We are returning to the office next month.

senses.definition: present participle of return: giving or sending something back
examples: I am returning the shoes because they do not fit.
They are returning the documents to the client.

senses.definition: not allowing things to be given or sent back
examples: All clearance items are sold on a returnless basis.
They described the decision as a returnless step into the future.

senses.definition: in a way that does not allow a return
examples: The river flowed returnlessly toward the sea.
She moved returnlessly into a new phase of her life.

senses.definition: third person singular of return: goes or comes back to a place or person
examples: He returns home late every evening.
The train returns to the station at midnight.

senses.definition: third person singular of return: gives or sends something back
examples: She always returns my calls quickly.
The shop returns the deposit after the equipment is brought back.

senses.definition: plural of return: profits, results, or official reports that come back
examples: Investors are pleased with the high returns this year.
Election returns will be announced later tonight.

senses.definition: describing a leaf or similar shape that is rounded at the end but has a small notch in the center
examples: The plant has small, retuse leaves at the tips of its stems.
Botanists noted the retuse form of the petals.

senses.definition: to twist or wind something around again
examples: She had to retwine the vines around the garden arch.
They carefully retwined the cable to prevent knots.

senses.definition: past tense and past participle of retwine: twisted or wound again
examples: They retwined the ropes around the post.
The stems were retwined to support the climbing plant.
